summary:
The conversion of Hungary to Christianity (c. 972–1000 AD) was the process by which the Magyar people — nomadic horse warriors from Central Asia who had settled in the Carpathian Basin in 895 AD and terrorized Europe with raids into France, Italy, and Germany for decades — transformed into a Christian European kingdom under King Stephen I (canonized as St. Stephen). The conversion was both a genuine religious change and a shrewd political calculation: Stephen (baptized Vajk, 975 AD) recognized that acceptance of Christianity was the price of integration into the European political community and protection from German military pressure.

Stephen I (r. 1000–1038 AD) received a royal crown from Pope Sylvester II in the year 1000 AD (the 'Crown of St. Stephen' became the sacred symbol of Hungarian statehood for a millennium), organized the Catholic church structure in Hungary with dioceses and monasteries, issued law codes, and systematically dismantled the tribal power of pagan Magyar chieftains. His political achievement was to convert a nomadic confederation into a stable feudal Christian kingdom capable of surviving in the competitive European state system. Hungary's conversion ended the era of Magyar raiding (the Battle of Lechfeld, 955 AD, had already broken Magyar military power in Germany) and opened Hungary to Western European cultural, religious, and economic integration. The Crown of St. Stephen remained the constitutional symbol of Hungarian sovereignty until 1946.
